cards.grammarNote: 은/는 marks the topic of a sentence — what the sentence is about. 은 after consonant, 는 after vowel. It often implies contrast or general statements: 저는 선생님이에요 = As for me, I'm a teacher.
이/가 marks the grammatical subject — the doer of an action. 이 after consonant, 가 after vowel. Used for new information or emphasis: 누가 의사예요? Who is the doctor? 제가요 = It's me.
저는 = I (topic marker 는 after 저). 는 sets 저 as the topic. 선생님이에요 = am a teacher (ends in consonant → 이에요).
제가 = I (subject marker 가 after 저→제 in humble speech). 가 emphasizes that I am specifically the one. 의사 ends in vowel → 예요.
무슨 = what kind of. 일 = work. 을 = object marker (consonant-ending noun). 해요 = do. 일을 하다 = to do work.
직업이 = job (subject marker 이 after consonant). 이/가 used in questions about preferences — the subject marker introduces the thing being evaluated.
직업이 = job + subject marker 이. 뭐예요 = what is it? 직업이 뭐예요? is the natural way to ask someone's profession.
취미 ends in vowel ㅣ → subject marker 가. 취미가 뭐예요? = What is your hobby? 취미는 (topic marker) can also be used to ask about hobby as a topic.
그게 = 그것이 (that + subject marker). 제 = my (humble). 일이에요 = it's work (이에요 after consonant).
저는 = as for me (topic). 요리가 = cooking (subject of 좋다). 좋아요 with 이/가 marks the thing you like as subject — a set pattern in Korean.
일이 = work (subject marker 이). 힘들다 (to be hard/exhausting) → 힘들어요. ㄹ verb: 힘들 + 어요 = 힘들어요.

cards.front: Are you looking for a job?
cards.grammarNote: 일자리 = job opening/position. 를 = object marker (vowel-ending noun). 찾다 (to look for) → 찾아요. 이력서 = résumé.
